name: AWS Middle East (UAE) Region
geopoliticalNotes: AWS's first full region in the UAE, providing local data residency for government and financial sectors, reducing reliance on foreign hubs.
notes: Spans multiple availability zones across Dubai and Abu Dhabi.

name: Microsoft Azure UAE North
geopoliticalNotes: Primary hub for Azure in the Middle East; part of the strategic partnership with G42 to pivot UAE's AI infrastructure towards US technology.
notes: Includes three availability zones for high availability.

name: Microsoft Azure UAE Central
geopoliticalNotes: Restricted region focused on government and disaster recovery for Dubai-based workloads, ensuring Abu Dhabi's data sovereignty.

name: Khazna AUH6 (Masdar City)
geopoliticalNotes: A critical node for G42's AI initiatives, hosting local compute for the Falcon LLM and supporting the UAE's National AI Strategy.
notes: Located in the Masdar City sustainable tech hub.

name: Khazna AUH5 (KIZAD)
geopoliticalNotes: Designed for high-density AI clusters; strategic location in the industrial zone to support national logistics and digital economy.
notes: Supports racks up to 60kW for AI workloads.

name: Khazna DXB1 (Al Meydan)
geopoliticalNotes: One of the foundational facilities for the UAE's domestic cloud strategy, serving as a primary site for government digital services.

name: Equinix DX1 Dubai IBX
geopoliticalNotes: Hosts the UAE-IX, the region's most significant internet exchange point, making it a critical node for international data traffic transit.
notes: Connectivity hub with 50+ network providers.

name: Equinix DX3 Dubai IBX
geopoliticalNotes: Expansion of Equinix's footprint to meet the surging demand for interconnected cloud services and edge computing in the Gulf.
notes: Located adjacent to DX1.

name: Equinix AD1 Abu Dhabi IBX
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic interconnection point in the capital, serving as a secondary hub for financial services and government agencies.

name: G42 AI Supercomputing Center
geopoliticalNotes: Home to the Artemis supercomputer; the technological core of the UAE's push for AI sovereignty and its strategic pivot away from Chinese hardware in favor of NVIDIA and Microsoft.
notes: Highly secure facility focused on massive AI model training.

name: Equinix SP4
geopoliticalNotes: Most connected data center in Latin America; houses the core of IX.br (São Paulo), one of the world's largest internet exchange points.
notes: Strategically located in the Tamboré/Barueri tech hub.

name: Ascenty Vinhedo Campus
geopoliticalNotes: Largest data center campus in Latin America; critical infrastructure hub for major US hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, Oracle).
notes: Located in the Campinas metropolitan area.

name: Scala Tamboré Campus
geopoliticalNotes: Major AI-ready hyperscale campus; home to SP4, the largest vertical data center in Latin America.
notes: Focuses on high-density workloads and sustainability.

name: Equinix SP3
geopoliticalNotes: Massive colocation site providing geographic redundancy and direct fiber links to the connectivity hub at SP4.

name: AWS South America Region
geopoliticalNotes: Most mature and feature-rich cloud region in South America; anchor for the digital economy and startup ecosystem in Brazil.

name: Azure Brazil South
geopoliticalNotes: Critical hub for corporate Brazil and highly regulated industries; paired with Rio for national disaster recovery.

name: GCP South America Region
geopoliticalNotes: Essential for low-latency access to Google's global network; major driver for data-heavy industries and retail in Latin America.

name: Equinix RJ2
geopoliticalNotes: Most important carrier-neutral facility in Rio de Janeiro; primary landing point for subsea cables and geographic redundancy for São Paulo.

name: Santander Campinas Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: Anchor for the financial sector in the interior of São Paulo state; established Campinas as a major technological hub.

name: Ascenty São Paulo 2
geopoliticalNotes: Key site for high-frequency trading and corporate headquarters in São Paulo's financial district due to ultra-low latency.

name: Alibaba Cloud Zhangbei Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: One of the world's largest intelligent computing centers (12 EFLOPS). It is the primary processing hub for Alibaba’s AI and e-commerce operations, serving as a critical node in the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei digital economy strategy.
notes: Highly energy-efficient facility using free-air cooling.

name: Tencent Gui'an Seven-Star Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: Known as China's most secure data center, partially built into a hill for maximum physical protection and disaster recovery. It stores Tencent's most sensitive core data and is a key part of national digital sovereignty.
notes: Often referred to as the 'Bomb Shelter' data center.

name: Huawei Cloud Gui'an Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: Huawei's largest global cloud data center, anchoring the Guizhou national computing hub. It is a cornerstone of the 'East Data, West Computing' project, aiming to rebalance China's digital infrastructure.
notes: Capacity for 1 million servers.

name: Alibaba Cloud Ulanqab Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: A massive facility in the 'Grassland Cloud Valley,' strategically located to provide low-cost, green-energy-powered computing for the Beijing market.
notes: Uses free-air cooling for 10 months of the year.

name: Huawei Cloud Ulanqab Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: Huawei's largest cloud data center in North China. Serves as a national backup center and a key node for the digital transformation of northern China's industrial base.
notes: Supports over 300,000 servers.

name: Tencent Tianjin High-tech CDC
geopoliticalNotes: AI-focused campus that underpins Tencent's large-model development (Hunyuan). Provides critical, low-latency AI inference capabilities for government and enterprise in the capital region.
notes: Part of Tencent's nationwide AI infrastructure network.

name: National Supercomputing Center in Wuxi
geopoliticalNotes: Houses the Sunway TaihuLight, built entirely with Chinese-designed processors. It is a symbol of China's push for technological self-reliance and is used for sensitive national defense research.
notes: World's fastest supercomputer from 2016-2018.

name: National Supercomputing Center in Guangzhou
geopoliticalNotes: Houses the Tianhe-2 (MilkyWay-2). It is a critical innovation platform for the Greater Bay Area, connecting the R&D ecosystems of Guangzhou, Shenzhen, and Hong Kong.
notes: Consistently ranked among the top supercomputers globally.

name: Tencent Qingyuan Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: The largest data center cluster in South China, housing over 1 million servers. It is the primary cloud hub for the Greater Bay Area and influences digital integration across the region.
notes: Uses Tencent's 4th-generation T-block modular technology.

name: Huawei Langfang Cloud Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: A strategic cloud hub for North China, positioned to balance lower operational costs in Hebei with high-speed connectivity to the Beijing market.
notes: Serves as a major production base for Huawei's consumer and enterprise cloud services.

name: Equinix FR2 Frankfurt IBX
geopoliticalNotes: A critical interconnection hub in Frankfurt, serving as a primary node for the DE-CIX internet exchange. Its central location makes it vital for European financial data traffic.
notes: Houses a large number of financial services and network providers.

name: AWS Frankfurt (FRA50)
geopoliticalNotes: Core facility for AWS's German region, essential for providing data sovereignty to German enterprises and government agencies under GDPR.
notes: Primary Availability Zone for eu-central-1.

name: Google Cloud Hanau Campus
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic expansion of Google's German footprint outside Frankfurt city center to bypass power constraints while maintaining low latency to DE-CIX.
notes: Part of Google's €1 billion investment in Germany.

name: Digital Realty FRA18 (Hanauer Landstraße)
geopoliticalNotes: Located on the Hanauer Landstraße 'data alley', this facility is AI-optimized and serves as a major node for cloud service providers and financial institutions.
notes: Highly interconnected site with direct access to hundreds of carriers.

name: NTT Frankfurt 1 Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: A flagship facility for NTT in Europe, providing critical infrastructure for Japanese and international enterprises operating within the EU's regulatory framework.
notes: One of the largest colocation campuses in Germany.

name: Microsoft Azure Germany West Central
geopoliticalNotes: Primary hub for Microsoft's sovereign cloud offerings in Germany, designed to meet the strict compliance requirements of the German public sector and highly regulated industries.
notes: Supports three availability zones in the Frankfurt region.

name: Google Dietzenbach Campus
geopoliticalNotes: Part of a massive €5.5 billion investment to expand Google's cloud infrastructure in Germany, incorporating waste-heat recovery to support local sustainability goals.
notes: Expected to be a major hub for AI and cloud services in the late 2020s.

name: Equinix FR5 Frankfurt IBX
geopoliticalNotes: Located in the Kleyerstraße carrier hotel, this facility is one of the most densely interconnected points in Europe, serving as a gateway for global data transit.
notes: Key site for network density and peering.

name: AWS Frankfurt (FRA52)
geopoliticalNotes: Another critical component of the AWS Frankfurt region, ensuring high availability and redundancy for cloud services within the European Union.
notes: Located in the eastern part of Frankfurt.

name: Digital Realty FRA10 (Ostend)
geopoliticalNotes: Host to the DE-CIX management and core infrastructure, making it arguably the most strategically significant piece of internet infrastructure in Germany.
notes: The heart of German internet connectivity.

name: Digital Realty MRS3 Marseille
geopoliticalNotes: Housed in a former WWII German submarine base, this facility is a key landing point for multiple intercontinental subsea cables, making it a vital digital bridge between Europe, Africa, the Middle East, and Asia.
notes: Highly secure facility with significant historical context.

name: Equinix PA13x Meudon
geopoliticalNotes: A €350M facility specifically designed for hyperscale AI workloads. It strengthens France's position as a leader in European AI infrastructure and digital sovereignty.
notes: First xScale facility in the Paris region.

name: Equinix PA10 Saint-Denis
geopoliticalNotes: Part of the dense interconnection cluster in Saint-Denis, PA10 is critical for the French financial and telecommunications sectors, providing high-density peering for global carriers.
notes: Features innovative sustainable technologies like heat recovery.

name: AWS Paris Region (eu-west-3)
geopoliticalNotes: A critical enabler of digital sovereignty for French businesses and government, allowing data to remain on French soil while utilizing AWS's global cloud platform.
notes: Consists of multiple availability zones across the Paris metro area.

name: Digital Realty MRS6 Marseille
geopoliticalNotes: A planned 50 MW facility dedicated to AI workloads, requiring a massive power feed. It solidifies Marseille's role as a primary Mediterranean gateway for the 'Digital Silk Road'.
notes: Will be one of the largest and most power-dense facilities in the region.

sources.name: IDC Nova - Digital Realty Marseille Expansion

name: Digital Realty PAR8 La Courneuve
geopoliticalNotes: Located in the major data center cluster north of Paris, PAR8 is a core node for international cloud and network connectivity, hosting a large number of global carriers.
notes: Highly interconnected campus with multiple sister facilities.

name: Microsoft Azure France Central
geopoliticalNotes: The primary Azure region for France, essential for providing cloud services to the public sector and industries requiring high levels of data protection and local residency.
notes: Provides three availability zones for maximum resilience.

name: Google Cloud Paris (europe-west9)
geopoliticalNotes: Google's first cloud region in France, aimed at supporting the country's 'Cloud de Confiance' strategy and ensuring compliance with local data sovereignty laws.
notes: Supports three availability zones in the Paris region.

name: Scaleway AION Paris
geopoliticalNotes: A French-owned 'AI Gigafactory', AION is strategically significant for its use of European-developed hardware and its role in fostering an independent European AI ecosystem.
notes: Equipped with high-performance NVIDIA H100 GPU clusters.

name: Telehouse Marseille
geopoliticalNotes: A critical interconnection point in Marseille, providing high-density access to the subsea cables landing in the port area and serving as a backup hub for Digital Realty's facilities.
notes: Key site for international carriers and ISP peering.

name: Telehouse North
geopoliticalNotes: The 'heart' of the UK internet and Europe’s first purpose-built colocation facility. It hosts the London Internet Exchange (LINX) and is a vital node for transatlantic data traffic between North America and Europe.
notes: Most connected data center in Europe.

name: Equinix LD4
geopoliticalNotes: The premier financial trading hub in Europe. It hosts the matching engines for major global equities and FX trading platforms, making it central to the stability of the City of London’s global financial markets.
notes: Critical for low-latency financial services.

name: Global Switch London East
geopoliticalNotes: A massive wholesale hub for hyperscalers. Its ownership by Chinese investors has historically led to national security scrutiny and concerns over data sovereignty in the UK.
notes: Subject of significant divestment and security debates in the 2020s.

name: Telehouse North Two
geopoliticalNotes: A high-rise expansion of the Telehouse campus featuring its own 132kV primary substation. It reinforces the UK's position as a sovereign connectivity hub post-Brexit.
notes: One of the most advanced and power-secure facilities in London.

name: Equinix LD8 (Harbour Exchange)
geopoliticalNotes: A critical 'failover' and redundancy site for the London Docklands cluster. It hosts a dense concentration of financial services and cloud on-ramps for major providers.
notes: Formerly the Telecity Harbour Exchange (HEX) facility.

name: VIRTUS London5
geopoliticalNotes: A massive hyperscale facility on the Western Corridor. Represents the UK's strategic move toward AI-ready compute capacity and sovereign data storage for enterprise and government.
notes: Part of the Stockley Park campus.

name: Global Switch London North
geopoliticalNotes: A legacy site that established the Docklands as a global nexus. Historically served as the termination point for the AC1 transatlantic cable linking the UK and US.
notes: Housed in a former printing works.

name: Digital Realty Sovereign House
geopoliticalNotes: Hosts AWS Direct Connect and LINX. It is a primary choice for firms requiring private access to global cloud tools while maintaining UK data under local jurisdiction.
notes: A key interconnection hub in the Docklands area.

name: Equinix LD6
geopoliticalNotes: Flagship facility in the Slough campus that provides massive fiber cross-connect capacity linking various financial exchanges, ensuring the resilience of the UK's financial digital ecosystem.
notes: Highly energy-efficient facility with LEED Gold certification.

name: VIRTUS London10
geopoliticalNotes: Part of the Slough Cluster, which is recognized as Critical National Infrastructure (CNI) by the UK government. It contributes to the largest data center hub in Europe by power capacity.
notes: Large-scale carrier-neutral colocation facility.

name: Yotta NM1
geopoliticalNotes: Largest Tier IV certified data center in Asia; critical for India's data sovereignty and hosting government workloads.
notes: Located in Panvel Data Center Park.

name: Equinix MB2
geopoliticalNotes: Most interconnected data center in India; hosts major internet exchanges (NIXI, DE-CIX) and financial ecosystem core.
notes: Acquired from GPX Global Systems in 2021.

name: NTT Mumbai 8
geopoliticalNotes: Part of NTT's massive Chandivali/Mahape campus; strategically critical for global hyperscale traffic overflow from central Mumbai.

name: Digital Connexion MAA10
geopoliticalNotes: First phase of 100MW campus; strategic gateway for subsea cables connecting India to Southeast Asia.
notes: Joint venture between Digital Realty, Brookfield, and Reliance Industries.

name: STT GDC Chennai
geopoliticalNotes: Critical hub for MIST subsea cable landing; facilitates low-latency connection to Malaysia and Singapore.
notes: Hosts major international subsea cable terminations.

name: AWS Hyderabad Region
geopoliticalNotes: India's second AWS region; provides disaster recovery for Mumbai and supports data residency for public sector clients.
notes: Region consists of 3 Availability Zones.

name: Microsoft Azure South India
geopoliticalNotes: Primary Azure hub for Southern India; essential for regional government and financial sector digital transformation.

name: Google Cloud Delhi Region
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic gateway for Northern India; critical for serving Indian Government's digital initiatives in the capital region.

name: CtrlS Hyderabad DC2
geopoliticalNotes: World's first LEED Platinum-certified Tier IV data center; hosts mission-critical infrastructure for India's largest banks.

name: STT GDC Bengaluru KML
geopoliticalNotes: Infrastructure hub for India's tech capital; hosts R&D environments for global capability centers (GCCs).

name: AWS Israel (Tel Aviv) Region
geopoliticalNotes: Result of the government's Project Nimbus; ensures critical state services run on local infrastructure to prevent foreign 'kill-switch' risks.
notes: Investment of $7.2 billion over 15 years.

name: Microsoft Azure Israel Central
geopoliticalNotes: Includes three availability zones; designed with high-security underground specifications to withstand regional kinetic threats.

name: Google Cloud Israel Region
geopoliticalNotes: Part of Project Nimbus; strategic hub for Google's Blue-Raman subsea cable system connecting Europe to India via Israel.

name: Oracle Cloud Jerusalem Region
geopoliticalNotes: A 9-floor underground bunker facility designed for extreme resilience; hosts critical government and security workloads in the capital.
notes: Located in the Har Hotzvim tech park.

name: MedOne Tirat Carmel (Northern Hub)
geopoliticalNotes: Major landing point for international submarine cables; acts as a critical connectivity gateway for Israel to the global internet.
notes: Underground, missile-hardened facility.

name: MedOne Petah Tikva
geopoliticalNotes: Located in Israel's primary 'Data Center Alley'; serves as a major carrier-neutral interconnection hub for local enterprises and global tech firms.

name: MedOne Ramla (Bunker)
geopoliticalNotes: Deep underground facility designed for maximum physical security and autonomous operation during national emergencies.

name: EdgeConneX Rishon LeTsiyon (IL-01)
geopoliticalNotes: Part of the global EdgeConneX network; provides high-security, carrier-neutral hosting in the central district.

name: EdgeConneX Herzliya (IL-02)
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic location in the Herzliya Pituach high-tech hub, serving many of Israel's leading technology and R&D companies.

name: Techtonic Titan 4 (Beit Shemesh)
geopoliticalNotes: A Tier IV underground facility designed specifically for AI workloads; part of the national push to host high-density compute safely beneath regional threats.
notes: Designed to withstand a direct missile strike.

name: AT TOKYO Chuo Center (CC1)
geopoliticalNotes: Japan's premier connectivity hub and heart of Tokyo's financial network. Hosting major internet exchanges (IXs) and providing high-security infrastructure designed to withstand major seismic events.
notes: One of the largest and most connected data centers in Japan.

name: AirTrunk TOK1
geopoliticalNotes: The largest hyperscale campus in Japan. A critical anchor for US cloud giants (AWS, Google, Azure) in the 'Inzai Data Center Park' cluster.

name: Equinix Tokyo TY11
geopoliticalNotes: A vital interconnection point for the global financial ecosystem in Tokyo, providing direct access to 85+ financial service providers and key stock exchanges.

name: Digital Realty KIX11 (Digital Osaka 2)
geopoliticalNotes: The primary gateway for international data exchange in Western Japan, serving as critical disaster recovery hub for Tokyo-based enterprises.

name: SoftBank Hokkaido Tomakomai AI Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically decentralizes AI compute away from Tokyo to Hokkaido, leveraging the cool climate and renewable energy for national resilience.
notes: Part of Japan's 'Brain DataCenter' initiative.

sources.name: SoftBank AI Data Center in Hokkaido

name: NTT Inzai Shiroi (TKY11)
geopoliticalNotes: A symbol of Japanese data sovereignty, ensuring domestic control over critical AI and cloud infrastructure in partnership with TEPCO.

name: Google Inzai Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: Google's first self-owned data center in Japan, serving as a landing point for the Topaz subsea cable connecting Japan to Canada.

name: Microsoft Japan East (Saitama Campus)
geopoliticalNotes: Primary site for Microsoft's collaboration with the Japanese government for hosting sensitive national-level AI and public sector workloads.

name: Colt Tokyo Inzai 3
geopoliticalNotes: Reinforces Japan's role as a stable, low-latency relay hub for global financial institutions and hyperscalers in the APAC region.

name: MC Digital Realty KIX13
geopoliticalNotes: An AI-ready facility in Western Japan supporting the digital transformation of the Kansai manufacturing belt and regional business connectivity.

name: Naver Gak Sejong
geopoliticalNotes: Asia's largest hyperscale data center and the cornerstone of South Korea's 'Sovereign AI' strategy, ensuring data independence from global hyperscalers.
notes: Features advanced robotics and eco-friendly cooling.

name: LG Uplus Pyeongchon Mega Center
geopoliticalNotes: One of the largest colocation facilities in Korea, serving as a critical hub for global content providers and financial institutions in the Seoul area.

name: Naver Gak Chuncheon
geopoliticalNotes: Naver's first purpose-built data center, demonstrating Korea's ability to build eco-friendly infrastructure leveraging natural mountain cooling.

name: Kakao Ansan Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: Built to ensure disaster resilience and data sovereignty for Korea's dominant messaging platform, KakaoTalk, following a major service outage in 2022.

name: Digital Edge SEL2
geopoliticalNotes: A vital landing point for international subsea cables in Incheon, connecting Korea's digital economy to the broader APAC region.

name: KT Mokdong IDC
geopoliticalNotes: The primary internet exchange hub for South Korea, handling the majority of domestic internet traffic and serving as a central node for government connectivity.

name: SK Broadband Gasan DC
geopoliticalNotes: A key hyperscale facility supporting the 5G and AI infrastructure of SK Telecom, one of the world's most advanced mobile operators.

name: Digital Realty ICN10
geopoliticalNotes: The first carrier-neutral data center in South Korea, breaking the traditional telco monopoly and facilitating global enterprise entry into the Korean market.
notes: NVIDIA DGX-Ready facility.

name: Equinix SL1
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically located in Sangam Digital Media City, acting as a gateway for global financial and tech firms to interconnect with the Korean ecosystem.

name: Samsung SDS Sangam IT Center
geopoliticalNotes: The high-tech hub for Samsung Group's global IT operations, representing the integration of industrial manufacturing and digital twins.

name: YTL Green DC Park
geopoliticalNotes: Major AI-ready data center campus hosting NVIDIA AI infrastructure; central to Malaysia's 'Sovereign AI' strategy and energy transition.
notes: First solar-powered data center park in Malaysia.

name: AWS Malaysia Region (ap-southeast-5)
geopoliticalNotes: AWS's first infrastructure region in Malaysia; part of a $6B long-term investment supporting the nation's Madani Economy vision.
notes: Consists of 3 Availability Zones.

name: Google Elmina Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: Google's first data center and cloud region in Malaysia; $2B investment reinforcing Malaysia's position as a regional tech hub.
notes: Located in Elmina Business Park.

name: Microsoft Malaysia West (Region Hub)
geopoliticalNotes: Microsoft's Bersama Malaysia initiative; critical for government and enterprise digital transformation and data residency.
notes: Consists of 3 Availability Zones.

name: NTT Cyberjaya 6 (CBJ6)
geopoliticalNotes: Sixth facility in NTT's Cyberjaya campus; a cornerstone of Malaysia's established digital hub for international enterprises.
notes: Supports high-density computing workloads.

name: Equinix JH1
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically located in Johor to capture spillover from Singapore's data center market; key node for cross-border digital services.
notes: Located in Nusajaya Tech Park.

name: Bridge DC MY03 (Johor)
geopoliticalNotes: Major hyperscale campus in Johor, serving global cloud and content providers; highlights Johor's emergence as a top-tier global DC hub.
notes: Located in Sedenak Tech Park.

name: GDS Nusajaya Campus
geopoliticalNotes: Investment by a major Chinese data center operator; illustrates Malaysia's neutral role attracting both Western and Chinese tech capital.
notes: First phase of a multi-building campus.

name: AirTrunk JHB1
geopoliticalNotes: Massive hyperscale campus designed to serve the scale requirements of major cloud providers in the region.
notes: Aims for high energy efficiency and sustainability.

name: TM One KVDC (Klang Valley Data Centre)
geopoliticalNotes: National infrastructure provided by the state-linked telecom; critical for sovereign government data and digital services.
notes: Certified Tier III by Uptime Institute.

name: Equinix AM4 Amsterdam IBX
geopoliticalNotes: A landmark vertical data center in Amsterdam Science Park. It handles ~38% of all Dutch data traffic and provides low-latency access to 80% of Europe, making it a critical node for global internet connectivity.
notes: Houses a significant portion of the AMS-IX infrastructure.

name: Microsoft Middenmeer Campus
geopoliticalNotes: A massive hyperscale campus that serves as the heart of Microsoft's Azure West Europe region. Its location in Agriport A7 is strategically chosen for power availability and cooling efficiency.
notes: One of Microsoft's largest data center footprints globally.

name: Google Eemshaven Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically located at a major subsea cable landing point, this facility is powered by 100% renewable energy from nearby offshore wind farms, reflecting the intersection of digital and energy geopolitics.
notes: Google's first 100% renewable energy data center.

name: Digital Realty AMS11 Schiphol
geopoliticalNotes: An AI-optimized facility near Schiphol Airport, featuring direct liquid cooling for high-density workloads. It strengthens Amsterdam's position as a key AI hub in Northern Europe.
notes: Strategically located near major logistics and transportation hubs.

name: Google Middenmeer Campus
geopoliticalNotes: Part of Google's multi-billion euro investment in the Netherlands, this campus provides critical redundancy for the Eemshaven site and supports the growing demand for cloud services in the Benelux region.
notes: Highly efficient facility using advanced cooling techniques.

name: Equinix AM3 Amsterdam Science Park
geopoliticalNotes: A key interconnection site in Amsterdam Science Park, providing high-density access to hundreds of carriers and ISPs, and serving as a major node for the NL-ix internet exchange.
notes: Known for its high network density and carrier-neutrality.

name: Digital Realty AMS9 Science Park
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically located in Amsterdam Science Park, this facility is a core node for international cloud and network connectivity, hosting a large number of global carriers and content providers.
notes: Highly interconnected campus with multiple sister facilities in the Amsterdam area.

name: Iron Mountain AMS1 Amsterdam
geopoliticalNotes: A major colocation facility in Amsterdam, providing secure and compliant infrastructure for enterprises and government agencies requiring high levels of data protection.
notes: Features extensive security measures and compliance certifications.

name: CyrusOne Amsterdam I
geopoliticalNotes: One of the largest data center developments in the Amsterdam region, this facility provides massive scale for cloud service providers and large enterprises operating in Europe.
notes: Located in the Polanen Park area, north of Amsterdam.

name: NorthC Amsterdam
geopoliticalNotes: A leading regional data center provider in the Netherlands, providing critical infrastructure for local businesses and public sector organizations, emphasizing regional data sovereignty.
notes: Focuses on providing localized cloud and colocation services.

name: Atman WAW-1 Campus
geopoliticalNotes: Largest domestic data center campus in Poland; primary hub for local internet traffic and a key node in the 'Cloud Valley' of Central Europe.
notes: Carrier-neutral; located in Praga district.

name: Atman WAW-3 Campus
geopoliticalNotes: New AI-ready hyperscale campus designed to meet the growing demand from global cloud providers in the CEE region.
notes: Located just outside Warsaw in Ożarów Mazowiecki.

name: Equinix WA4x (xScale)
geopoliticalNotes: Poland's first xScale facility, catering to hyperscale cloud providers; solidifies Warsaw's role as a strategic cloud bridge between West and East.
notes: Powered by 100% renewable energy.

name: Vantage WAW1 Campus
geopoliticalNotes: Major investment by a US-based hyperscale specialist, supporting Poland's rapid digital transformation and cloud adoption.
notes: Located in the Bielany district.

name: Data4 Warsaw Campus
geopoliticalNotes: Part of a major European expansion by Data4; provides resilient infrastructure for international cloud and enterprise clients in Poland.
notes: Target capacity of 60MW across multiple buildings.

name: Equinix WA1 (LIM Building)
geopoliticalNotes: The most connected building in Poland; home to the Equinix Internet Exchange and a critical hub for regional connectivity.
notes: Located in the Marriott (LIM) building in central Warsaw.

name: Microsoft Poland Central (Region Hub)
geopoliticalNotes: Microsoft's first cloud region in CEE; represents a $1B investment in Poland's digital infrastructure and national security.
notes: Consists of 3 Availability Zones across Warsaw area.

name: Google europe-central2 (Region Hub)
geopoliticalNotes: First Google Cloud region in CEE; a critical safe haven for Ukrainian data during the ongoing conflict.
notes: Consists of 3 Availability Zones.

name: Atman WAW-2 (Konstruktorska)
geopoliticalNotes: Key connectivity node in Warsaw's business district; hosts the Thinx Internet Exchange.
notes: Located in the Mokotów business hub.

name: Equinix WA3
geopoliticalNotes: Supports international businesses and the growing demand for digital ecosystems in the CEE region.
notes: Located in the Salomea district.

name: IXcellerate MOS5 (Moscow South)
geopoliticalNotes: Largest carrier-neutral commercial facility in Russia; critical for financial and retail sectors amidst shift to domestic cloud infrastructure.
notes: Located in the Biryulyovo Zapadnoye district.

name: Yandex Kaluga (Grabtsevo)
geopoliticalNotes: Core infrastructure for Yandex Cloud and AI training; central to Russia's digital sovereignty and domestic AI development.
notes: Designed for high-density AI workloads.

name: Sberbank Balakovo
geopoliticalNotes: Massive regional AI data center supporting GigaChat and Sber's financial ecosystem; part of Russia's push for regional digital development.
notes: Phase 1 operational; total planned capacity up to 120MW.

name: Rostelecom Udomlya
geopoliticalNotes: Strategically located next to the Kalinin Nuclear Power Plant for stable, low-cost power; hosts government and critical state data.
notes: Co-project with Rosatom.

name: Rosatom Moscow-2
geopoliticalNotes: Tier IV certified facility acquired by Rosatom to expand state-controlled cloud and storage services.
notes: Located on Dorozhnaya Street.

name: Sberbank Skolkovo (MDC-2)
geopoliticalNotes: Flagship facility in the Skolkovo Innovation Center; central hub for Russia's largest banking and digital ecosystem.
notes: One of the largest in Europe at launch.

name: IXcellerate MOS3 (Moscow North)
geopoliticalNotes: Major connectivity hub in Northern Moscow; critical for international enterprises maintaining presence in Russia.
notes: Carrier-neutral facility.

name: Rostelecom Medvedkovo
geopoliticalNotes: Large-scale facility acquired to support growing demand for sovereign data storage and state services.
notes: Acquired from 3Data/PNK Group.

name: DataPro Moscow I
geopoliticalNotes: One of the most connected and reliable commercial facilities in Moscow; hosts major enterprise and financial clients.
notes: Located on Aviamotornaya Street.

name: Selectel Yurlovsky
geopoliticalNotes: High-density facility designed to Tier IV standards; supports Selectel's growing cloud and bare-metal services as Western providers exit.
notes: Located in Yurlovsky Proyezd.

name: Center3 AI Data Center (Riyadh)
geopoliticalNotes: A massive 200MW facility dedicated to AI workloads; a cornerstone of Vision 2030 to position Saudi Arabia as the regional leader in AI and data processing.
notes: Part of the multi-billion dollar STC data center expansion project.

name: Center3 RDC-05 (Mursalat)
geopoliticalNotes: Core facility for government digital transformation and national security communications; located at the main STC campus.

name: Mobily Malga 2 Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: One of the few Tier IV certified (Fault Tolerant) facilities in the Kingdom, hosting critical infrastructure for Aramco and major financial institutions.
notes: First Tier IV facility in the region.

name: Google Cloud Region Saudi Arabia
geopoliticalNotes: Google's first region in the Kingdom, operated through a JV with Aramco (CNTXT), signaling a major shift in tech partnership towards US hyperscalers.
notes: Focuses on providing AI and data analytics for the energy sector.

name: Microsoft Azure Saudi Arabia East
geopoliticalNotes: Major investment aimed at supporting sovereign cloud requirements for the Saudi government; strategically located near the Dammam subsea cable landing points.
notes: Targeting 100MW of capacity upon completion.

name: Oracle Cloud Riyadh Region
geopoliticalNotes: A $1.5 billion investment to support the Kingdom's booming AI and cloud demand, fulfilling strict data residency laws for public sector data.
notes: Second Oracle region in the Kingdom.

name: Oracle Cloud Jeddah Region
geopoliticalNotes: The first major hyperscale region in Saudi Arabia, enabling local hosting for Western Region enterprises and government entities.

name: Center3 MENA Gateway 1 (MG1)
geopoliticalNotes: A critical landing point for major subsea cables (including 2Africa), serving as the 'Western Gateway' for data traffic between Europe and Asia.
notes: Highly interconnected site with major carrier PoPs.

name: Huawei Cloud Riyadh Region
geopoliticalNotes: Huawei's flagship region in the Middle East; represents China's significant digital footprint in the Kingdom despite US pressure to limit Huawei's influence.
notes: Provides local cloud services for Chinese firms operating in KSA.

name: Center3 RDC-102 (Remal)
geopoliticalNotes: Part of the strategic hyperscale cluster in the capital to support the exponential growth of the Saudi digital economy.

name: Equinix SG1
geopoliticalNotes: A critical interconnection hub in Southeast Asia, hosting multiple internet exchanges and subsea cable landings, facilitating regional data transit.
notes: Located in the Ayer Rajah Crescent hub.

name: Equinix SG3
geopoliticalNotes: One of the largest colocation facilities in Singapore, serving as a key node for global financial services and cloud service providers.
notes: Located in the Buona Vista/One-North research and tech district.

name: Google Singapore SIN-1
geopoliticalNotes: Google's first multi-story data center in Asia, critical for serving Southeast Asian markets and ensuring data residency within Singapore's stable jurisdiction.
notes: Part of a large Google campus in Jurong West.

name: AWS Singapore Region (SIN-1)
geopoliticalNotes: The primary cloud hub for Southeast Asia, providing sovereign compute capabilities for regional governments and major enterprises.
notes: Representing a major AZ site in the Jurong industrial area.

name: Digital Realty SIN10
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic colocation site in the Jurong cluster, providing critical redundancy and connectivity for Western cloud giants in the APAC region.

name: Digital Realty SIN11 (Digital Loyang 1)
geopoliticalNotes: Located near the Loyang subsea cable landing stations, this facility is a vital gateway for trans-pacific and regional data traffic.
notes: Located in the Loyang industrial estate.

name: Meta Singapore Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: Meta's first custom-built data center in Asia, centralizing user data for billions across the region and emphasizing Singapore's role as a trusted data vault.
notes: An 11-story building, one of the first of its kind in the world.

name: STT Defu 3
geopoliticalNotes: A homegrown champion representing Singapore's domestic capabilities in high-security, carrier-neutral colocation infrastructure.

name: NTT DATA Serangoon Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: A massive hyperscale AI campus, supporting the next generation of sovereign AI compute for the region.

name: Singtel Nxera DC West
geopoliticalNotes: A flagship AI-ready data center by Singapore's national telco, integrated with NVIDIA technology to support national AI initiatives.

name: Turkcell Gebze Data Center
geopoliticalNotes: Largest data center in Turkey; central to Turkcell's enterprise cloud and national data sovereignty strategy.
notes: Located in Gebze, on the Asian side of Istanbul.

name: Equinix IL2 Istanbul
geopoliticalNotes: Most densely connected and carrier-neutral hub in Turkey; gateway for international cloud and financial services.
notes: Formerly a Zenium facility.

name: NGN Star of Bosphorus
geopoliticalNotes: Turkey's first seismic-isolated data center; critical for business continuity in a high-earthquake-risk region.
notes: Located in Tuzla, Istanbul.

name: Huawei Cloud Istanbul Region
geopoliticalNotes: First major global public cloud region launched locally in Turkey; reflects deepening China-Turkey tech cooperation.

name: Turkcell Ankara Temelli
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic hub in the capital; designated to host the future Google Cloud Turkey Region as a Sovereign Cloud.
notes: Critical for government data residency.

name: Türk Telekom Esenyurt
geopoliticalNotes: High-tech hub featuring liquid immersion cooling; critical node for national connectivity and international fiber backhaul.
notes: Located on the European side of Istanbul.

name: Khazna Ankara TUR1
geopoliticalNotes: Strategic UAE-Turkey joint venture; set to be Turkey's largest AI-ready hyperscale facility.
notes: Planned for the Başkent Organized Industrial Zone.

name: Vodafone Istanbul Esenyurt
geopoliticalNotes: Critical node for Vodafone's enterprise ecosystem and international subsea cable terminations in Turkey.

name: Equinix IL4 Istanbul
geopoliticalNotes: Optimized for high-density AI and hybrid cloud; provides direct on-ramps to global cloud providers from Istanbul.

name: Türk Telekom Ümitköy
geopoliticalNotes: Primary public sector and government data hub in the capital; essential national infrastructure for civil services.

name: Equinix Ashburn Campus (DC2)
geopoliticalNotes: Located in 'Data Center Alley,' where approximately 70% of global internet traffic passes through. Its proximity to Washington D.C. makes it a critical hub for federal government agencies and international data flows to Europe.
notes: Part of the massive Equinix Ashburn campus, the most interconnected hub in North America.

name: NAP of the Americas (Equinix MI1)
geopoliticalNotes: The primary gateway for data traffic between the U.S. and Latin America/Caribbean. It hosts the U.S. Department of State’s Diplomatic Telecommunications Service and connects to 15 subsea cables.
notes: One of the largest and most connected data centers in the world.

name: One Wilshire
geopoliticalNotes: The most important telecommunications hub on the West Coast and the primary terminus for fiber-optic cables connecting the U.S. to the Pacific Rim. One-third of all internet traffic between the U.S. and Asia passes through this building.
notes: The building was converted from an office tower to a major carrier hotel starting in the 1990s.

name: 60 Hudson Street (Carrier Hotel)
geopoliticalNotes: A premier 'Carrier Hotel' and the landing point for numerous transatlantic cables. It is a critical node for global financial markets (Wall Street) and serves as a major interconnection point for international telecommunications.
notes: Formerly the Western Union Building, now one of the most important hubs in the global internet backbone.

name: Lakeside Technology Center (350 East Cermak)
geopoliticalNotes: One of the world's largest data centers, serving as the central hub for the U.S. financial sector (CME Group) and the primary interconnection point for the Midwest.
notes: Housed in the former R.R. Donnelley printing plant.

name: Utah Data Center (Bumblehive)
geopoliticalNotes: Operated by the NSA, this facility is designed to store exabytes of data for the U.S. Intelligence Community. It is a key asset for national security and signal intelligence.
notes: The facility's power capacity is estimated at 65 MW.

name: Infomart Dallas
geopoliticalNotes: The dominant carrier-neutral hub in the Southern U.S., serving as a critical bridge for data traffic between the U.S. and Mexico and a major node for South American connectivity.
notes: Equinix acquired the Infomart building in 2018.

name: Switch Citadel Campus
geopoliticalNotes: The largest data center campus in the world. Its design emphasizes extreme physical security and 100% renewable energy, making it a strategic site for mission-critical hyperscale workloads and national-scale disaster recovery.
notes: The campus is designed to scale up to 7.2 million square feet and 650 MW of power.

name: 111 Eighth Avenue
geopoliticalNotes: One of the most network-dense buildings in the world. It serves as a primary hub for Google’s global services and a major interconnection point for the East Coast's financial and media sectors.
notes: Google purchased the building in 2010 and it serves as their NY headquarters and a major data center.

name: Digital Realty Hillsboro Campus
geopoliticalNotes: A critical 'Digital Gateway' to the Asia-Pacific region due to its proximity to several trans-Pacific subsea cable landings. It offers a low-latency, tax-advantaged alternative to Silicon Valley for major hyperscalers.
notes: Hillsboro has become one of the fastest-growing data center markets in the U.S.

name: Central Bank of Iraq Data Center
